California
SUPERSEDED
Fair Political
Practices Cornrnission
IN PART
March 26′  ■99■

92‐乞|?
Judith sproul Davis
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON,
PARRINELLO & MUELLER
770 L street, suite 8oo
Sacramento, cA 9 5814
Re: Your Requests ler Ia-fgrmal Assistance
our File Nos. (r-91--1ll and I-91-a43
Dear I'Is. Davis:
You have requested confirrnation of telephone advj-ce provided
to you concerning the nei., provisions of the Potitical Reforn Act
(the ,,Act',) 1/ IimitinS gifts and honoraria. since your guestions
are general in nature and you have not named a specj-f ic eLected
offi6iat on $/hose behalf you have requested this advice, we provide
the following informat responses to your questions. z/ Further,
since both your letters requestinq advice deal with the ner^, gift
and honoraria lirnits of the Act, we are providing this single
response to your requests .
Please be aware that the issues raised in this letter present
significant policy guestions which wilL be presented to the com-
niision for consideration in the near future- At that tine the
Comnission may consider the continuing appropriateness of the
existing regulations in light of the new ethics provisions.
OUESTIONS
1. If a person invites an elected state officer to make a
speech in californj,a and provj.des transportation, meals and lodging
in connection with the event, is the provisi-on of the
transportation, meal,s and lodging a I'gj,ftrr as defined in the Act?
l/ 
covernnent code sections 81ooo-91o15. AII statutory refer-
ences are to the Government code unl-ess otherwise indicated. 
Con-
rnission regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations
section 18ooo, et ses. A11 references to regulations are to
Title 2, oivision 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2/ Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor vrith the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114,' Regulation 18329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 80O . P.O. Box 8o7 o Sacramento cA 958o4-o8o7 . (916)322.5660




File No. I-9■―■■4 and I-9■―■43
Page 2
2. If a gift is nade to the spouse of a public official 
and
the official 
exercises no discreti-on or control over the use of the
gift, 
has the off icj.al, received a trgiftrr as defined in the Act?
3. If an official- is given a ticket to an event and suggests
that the donor also give a ticket to someone el-se, i/ouId the gift
of this second ticket also be a gj.ft to the official?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Payments to an elected state officer of intrastate travel
and reasonable accommodations in connection with a speech currently
are not considered payments, gifts or income under Regulation
l-8728 (a) and Regulation 18623 (a) . Therefore we are advising that
such payments are not disclosabfe or subject to the new gift or
honorlria Iimits of the Act, or the conflict-of- interest
provisions. However both of these regulations will be presented to
the commission for their review in the near future.
2. cifts to a public official's 
spouse from which the
officia] 
enjoys no benefits and over which the official 
has no
discretion or control are not discLosabte gifts to the officiaL.
3. If an official 
is able to exercj-se control over the gift,
the gift is likety to be deemed a gift to the of f icial-. 
If the
officia:. merely suggests that the donor make a gift to another
person, the reiulting gift would general-Iy not be a gift to the
Lffi"i"t 
sirnpty by virtue of the suggestion. However, r'rhether the
gift comes unaLr tne control of the officiat 
is necessarily a
iactuaL determination which nust be nade on a case-by-case basis.
DI SCUSSION
Effective January L, L99L, the Legislature enacted a series of
statutes that Linit or ban certain gifts or honoraria made to
public officials. 
The Act now provides:
No Ioca1 elected of f icehol-der shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meetj-ng, social event, meal, or like
gathering, or any gifts, fron any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000), in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual- traveL expenses and
reasonable subsistence i.n connection therer"/ith.
(a)
Section 8950■
.
No elected state offi9er may accept an
honorarium.
(b) rrHonorariun" means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any payment made in consideration


File NO. I-9■
―■■4 and I-9■―■43
Page 3
for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meetingr, social event, mea1, or like
gathering.
***
(f) 
For purposes of this section, rrelected
state off i,cerI includes the Insurance Comrnissioner.
rt 
Regulation 18623(a) (copy enclosed) provides a sj.milar
exception. Regulation 18623(a) provides: Eree adnission, food,
beverages and sirnj,lar norninal benefits provided to an eLected
state officer, legislative off icia1,... agency official, 
or state
candidate at an event at which he or she nakes a speech or
participates in a panel or seminar, and reirnbursenent or advance
for actual intrastate travel, and for necessary accommodations
provided directly in connection with the event are not gifts or
income.
Section 89502.
Hor"rever, while the new provisions of the Act modify the
existing provisions of the Act, they do not automatical-Iy repeal
or redeiine alt the existing regulations concerning gifts and
honoraria. Thus, for exampl-e, although the gift lirnits are new to
the Act, they are based on the existing definitions in the Act, as
interpreted through commission regulations.
1. Intrastate Travel and Accommodations
This l-etter' confj,rrns that your letter dated February 27,
1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on that date. Regulation 18728 (copy enclosed) provides:
( a ) As used in this section, trhonorar j'unrl
means q-pgy4gg! for speakj,ng at any event,
participatj.ng in a panel or seminar or engTaging in
lny similar activity. 
For purposes of this sec-
tion, free admission, food, beverages and sinilar
norninal benef j,ts provided to a filer at an event at
which he or she speaks, partici,pates in a panel or
serninar or perforrns a sirniLar service, and
reimbursement or advance for actual- intrastate
travel and for necessary accommodations provided
dLrectly in connection with the event are not pav-
ments and need not be reported by the fil-er.
(Emphasis added. 1 3 /
Travel and accommodations thich are not considered |tpafrmentsrl
under Regulation l-8728 (a) are not disclosed and do not trigger
conflicts of interest. 
Staff is currently advising that such


File No. I-91-1,L4 and I-91-143
Page 4
travel and accommodations are also not rrpaymentstr for purposes of
the new gift and honoraria lirnits. 
we have interpreted the
language of the new provisions to be consistent with Regulation
:-a728. (Koppes Advice Letter, No. I-9o-517, copy enclosed.)
Thus, r,rhere intrastate travel is provided in connectj-on with a
speech or with participation in a panel or seminar consistent with
RLgulation la72a, the travet need not be reported and is not
subject to the gift and honoraria limits, or the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.
Sirnilarly, under Regul,ation 18623(a) the provision of
intrastate travel and accommodations is not a gift or income-
consequently, the provision of Payments for intrastate travel and
accornrnodations within the strictures of Regulation 18623 (a) would
not be disclosable and not subject to the gift or honoraria limits
of the Act, or the Act's conf lict-of- interest provisions.
Horrever, both of the regul-ations discussed above will be
presented to the Cornmission in the near future for consideration
tt tneir continued viability 
in light of the new ethics
provisions.
2. ci-fts to other Fanilv Members
This letter also confirrns that your letter dated March 5,
1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on February 27, L99L concerning Regulation la'726- 2 and gifts to a
menbers of a public officiaf's 
family- Regulation L8726.2 (copy
enclosed) provides:
(a) Gifts given directly to menbers of an
official's 
irnrnediate farnily are not gifts to the
official 
unless used or disposed of by the official
or given by the recipient nernber of the official's
irnmediate fanily to the official 
for disposition or
use at the official's 
discretion.
(b) cifts delivered by maif or other written
communication are given dj,rectly to members of the
official's 
imrnediate family if the fanily members'
nanes or familial designations (such as rrspouserr)
appear in the address on the envelope or in the
cornmunication tendering or offering the gift, 
and
the gift is j-ntended for their use or enjoyrnent.
(c) A gift given to the official, 
but
designated for the official 
and spouse or farnily,
is a gift to the official 
if the official 
exercises
discretj.on and control over who will actual-1y use
the gift.
(d) If the official 
enjoys direct benefit
frorn a single gift, as well as nembers of the


file No. I-91-114 and I-91-l-43
Page 5
official's 
family, the full value of the gift is
attributable to the official.
Thus gifts to a pub]lc official's 
spouse from which the
official 
enjoys no benefits and over which the official 
has no
dj-scretion or control are not disclosable gifts subject to the
limits of the Act and do not trigger conflicts of interest.
3. Gifts to others at An official's 
Behest
In our conversations of February 28, 1991, and March l-, 1991,
i.re also discussed direction of gifts by officials to others. I
can only conf irrn the advice hrith a qualifier. 
rn those
conversations you asked $/hether a public official's 
rrsuggesti-onrr
to the donor that tickets to an event be given to other persons
would mean that the tickets would be treated as gifts to the
official 
if the donor in fact provided tickets to those persons.
I stated that generally they would not be treated as gifts to the
official. 
This letter confirrns that general conclusion. However,
I also cautioned that the conclusion $/as necessarily based on the
facts of the particular situation and that application of the
advice must be considered on a case-by-case basis.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should
have any further guestions regarding this natter, please feel
to contact me at (916) 322-59OL.
S incere Iy ,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counseL
